scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the contemporary jury procedures of the Athenian courts in the 4th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348) as he describes a contemporary Athenian legal procedure.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This rule concerns the standard payment of jurors within the Athenian democratic court system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes this as the current process in Athens during his lifetime, dated to his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the restriction of the archonship to the Eupatridae (nobles) in the 7th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text explicitly dates the transition to annual archons and Eupatridae eligibility to 682 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the wealth-based census classes and the exclusion of the lowest class (Thetes) from high office.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the constitutional property classes typically established or codified by Solon in 594 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text discusses a specific expansion of eligibility for the archonship in the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The passage explicitly provides the date 457 BCE for the archonship of Mnesitheides when the law changed.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Relates to the introduction and increase of payment for citizens attending the Athenian Assembly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text identifies the increase in pay by referencing a play performed specifically in 392 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Specifies the contemporary Athenian citizenship law requiring dual-citizen parentage.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle reports this as the current law of Athens during his own time, dated to his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the Athenian institution of the 'epikleros' (heiress), a fundamental part of their family law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes this as an active legal custom in his contemporary society, dated to his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Pertains to the dowry obligations and Archon's protection for Athenian heiresses.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle is documenting the current legal responsibilities of the Athenian Archon during his lifetime.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Descriptions of trierarchy and choregia relate to the fiscal obligations of wealthy Athenian citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects the liturgy system as it operated in Athens during Aristotle's time (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to the 'euthyna', the mandatory audit for Athenian officials at the end of their term.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This was a central contemporary feature of the Athenian democratic constitution described by Aristotle.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the ongoing legal protection provided by the Athenian state to female heiresses.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle presents this as the functioning law of the city in his own era (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the status of 'meticians' (resident aliens) in Athens and their need for a 'prostate' (patron).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle is recording the contemporary legal requirements for non-citizens living in 4th-century Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Concerns the mandatory service of Athenian men in their 60th year as public arbitrators.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This service was a current requirement of the Athenian civic cycle during Aristotle’s floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the Athenian legal action of 'antidosis' (property exchange) regarding public burdens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle documents this as an available legal mechanism in his own contemporary Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle theorizes the contemporary social and economic institution of slavery as it existed in the Greek world.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348), reflecting his justification of his era's social structures.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle articulates a Panhellenic view of Greek superiority over non-Greeks (barbarians).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects 4th-century BCE Greek ideological norms regarding foreigners, dated to the author's floruit.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author describes the patriarchal structure of the household (oikos) as a universal contemporary norm in Greece.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348); he uses Homer to justify a contemporary social hierarchy.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle defines the legal and economic status of slaves as property within contemporary Greek society.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary Athenian and broader Greek legal reality of slavery in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle explains the total legal dependency of the slave upon the master in his contemporary world.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This definition of slavery was the contemporary legal standard during Aristotle's floruit in -348.
